text: Abstract Here, we investigated whether chemical reactions in droplets could be carried out at a faster rate than that in conventional solution, utilizing the high resolution achieved by the mass spectrometer. We demonstrated through charged electrospray experiments the formation of isoquinoline in the charged electrospray droplet, and the complexation between cytochrome c and maltose in the fused water droplets. Marked acceleration of reactions was achieved, as evidenced by rates that are enhanced by at least a millionfold for the former, and by at least a thousandfold for the latter. Using microdroplets about 1–15 μm in diameter (0.5 pl to 2 nl) to catalyze reactions should therefore represent a generic approach to greatly enhance rates of reactions comprising both covalent and noncovalent processes. Several possible contributing effects are as yet not clear. In the context of fused water droplets we can here rule out (or strongly minimize) droplet evaporation given that the time of flight between droplet fusion and when the fused water droplet is injected into the heated capillary of the mass spectrometer is short and the evaporation is deemed to be negligible within this time frame. Thus our experiments seem to suggest two possible contributions for the accelerating effect of the fusion of water droplets in air—other possible contributing factors are of course not negated. We argue that the microdroplet chemistry, using mass spectrometry to detect the outcome of the reactions can open up a novel and useful area to study the catalysts, their mechanisms as well as to promote the occurrence of complex reactions or the transformation of difficult species.
